perturbation.original_text: To be considered Confidential Information, non-tangible disclosures must be identified as confidential prior to disclosure and produced in writing, marked as provided above and delivered to the receiving party within thirty (30) days of the original date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: To be considered Confidential Information, non-tangible disclosures must be identified as confidential prior to disclosure and produced in writing, marked as provided above and delivered to the receiving party within ninety (90) days of the original date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the deadline for delivering written confirmation of non-tangible disclosures from 30 to 90 days could be considered commercially unreasonable and creates ambiguity that could be exploited in litigation, undermining the confidentiality agreement. Thirty days is a standard period for such matters. Setting this longer period could be viewed as a breach of the duty to act in good faith and deal fairly in contractual relations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law, every contract contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Changing a standard 30-day deadline to 90 days for documenting confidential information may be considered unreasonable and could be seen as a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing as it can prejudice the other party's rights under the contract.

General Provisions Part 1 General Provisions Â§ 1-101. Short titles. (a) This subtitle may be cited as the Uniform Commercial Code. (b) This article may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code â General Provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â1-101;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-102. Scope of article. This article applies to a transaction to the extent that it is governed by another article of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-103. Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote its purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. (a) The Uniform Commercial Code must be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes and policies, which are: (1) To simplify, clarify, and modernize the law governing commercial transactions; (2) To permit the continued expansion of commercial practices through custom, usage, and agreement of the parties; and (3) To make uniform the law among the various jurisdictions. (b) Unless displaced by the particular provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the principles of law and equity, including the law merchant and the law relative to capacity to contract, principal and agent, estoppel, fraud, misrepresentation, duress, coercion, mistake, bankruptcy, and other validating or invalidating cause supplement its provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â1-102;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-104. Construction against implied repeal. The Uniform Commercial Code being a general act intended as a unified coverage of its subject matter, no part of it shall be deemed to be impliedly repealed by subsequent legislation if such construction can reasonably be avoided. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-105. Severability. If any provision or clause of the Uniform Commercial Code or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid, the invalidity does not affect other provisions or applications of the Uniform Commercial Code which can be given effect without the invalid provision or application, and to this end the provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code are severable. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-106. Use of singular and plural; gender. In the Uniform Commercial Code, unless the statutory context otherwise requires: (1) Words in the singular number include the plural, and those in the plural include the singular; and (2) Words of any gender also refer to any other gender. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-107. Section captions. Section captions are part of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-108. perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the deadline for providing written confirmation of confidential information from 30 to 90 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this change could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention deadlines for confidential information or the covenant of good faith, it outlines the UCC's purpose to 'simplify, clarify, and modernize the law governing commercial transactions' and to permit the expansion of commercial practices through custom, usage, and agreement of the parties'. The change from 30 to 90 days introduces ambiguity and potentially prejudices the other party, contradicting the principle of good faith implied in contracts, and deviating from standard commercial practices for confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a shorter period is indicated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not insert any claims of breach of this Agreement or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information made more than five <PAGE> 2 (5) years from the date of the CITR under which such information was disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a shorter period is indicated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not insert any claims of breach of this Agreement or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information made more than one (1) year from the date of the CITR under which such information was disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period for asserting claims of breach or misappropriation to one year may be insufficient under the statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation, particularly under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. Although the DTSA does not explicitly state a statute of limitations, case law often applies a statute of limitations that is longer than one year. By contractually limiting the claim period to one year, the agreement could conflict with the general understanding and application of trade secret law, potentially hindering a party's ability to pursue a claim for misappropriation within the legally recognized timeframe.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the claim period for breach or misappropriation to one year, while the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) typically operates with a longer statute of limitations. The provided snippet from 18 USC 1836 establishes civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation. By shortening the claim period, the modified text could prevent a party from pursuing a claim within the legally recognized timeframe, thus contradicting the intent and application of trade secret law. The modification removes a legally recognized right to pursue action within a reasonable (longer) timeframe, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause without written notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement of written notice for termination may be problematic, as proper notice is generally required for contract termination to be effective. This may lead to disputes about whether the agreement was properly terminated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles of Termination
perturbation.law_citation: General Contract Law Principles of Termination (Restatement (Second) of Contracts)
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law requires reasonable notification when terminating a contract, particularly when the contract involves ongoing obligations. Removing the written notice requirement contradicts the standard practice and legal expectation that termination requires clear communication to the other party, potentially leading to legal challenges regarding the validity of the termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-308. Performance or Acceptance Under Reservation of Rights. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-308. Performance or Acceptance Under Reservation of Rights. (a) A party that with explicit reservation of rights performs or promises performance or assents to performance in a manner demanded or offered by the other party does not thereby prejudice the rights reserved. Such words as "without prejudice," "under protest," or the like are sufficient. (b) Subsection (a) does not apply to an accord and satisfaction. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'written notice' requirement for contract termination, potentially contradicting standard contract law practices. The Law Explanation indicates that general contract law requires reasonable notification when terminating a contract, especially when ongoing obligations are involved. Removing written notice could lead to disputes over the validity of the termination. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent, expressed by a valid offer and acceptance'. While this snippet doesn't directly address the mode of communication for termination, the elimination of written notice introduces ambiguity around the clarity of 'mutual assent' regarding the termination. The deletion of a clear term that enforces how the contract is terminated creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Without prior written consent of the other party to this Agreement, neither party will for a period of eighteen (18) months from the Effective Date: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights or options to acquire any voting securities of the other party
perturbation.changed_text: Without prior written consent of the other party to this Agreement, neither party will for a period of three (3) months from the Effective Date: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights or options to acquire any voting securities of the other party
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the standstill period to three months is likely inadequate protection for DSP Communications, particularly in the context of a potential acquisition. This shorter period could run afoul of SEC regulations related to insider trading or market manipulation if one party uses confidential information gained during the discussions to trade the other party's stock shortly after the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. While the three-month standstill period might seem acceptable on the surface, if one party uses confidential information gained during the due diligence process to trade the other party's stock soon after the standstill expires, it could potentially violate Rule 10b-5. Insider trading or market manipulation based on the information gained is prohibited and three months could allow enough time for the confidential information's impact to still be relevant.
perturbation.justification: The change from an 18-month to a 3-month standstill period creates a potential legal contradiction under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction,' because it involves a time-sensitive obligation. The original text provided an 18-month standstill which would provide ample protection to DSP Communications. However, the new change reduces the period to three months, and the Law Explanation states that a three-month standstill period might not prevent potential violations of Rule 10b-5 concerning insider trading or market manipulation. This is because it might still allow sufficient time for the confidential information to still be relevant, making the contractual term unenforceable or illegal because it violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Confidential Information may be controlled by U.S. Export Regulations, and export, re-export or foreign disclosure (including to subsidiary employees) may require U.S. Government approval. The receiving party shall not use, export, transfer, make available or otherwise disclose any Confidential Information in violation of U.S. Export Regulations, including any use or development in nuclear, missile, chemical and/or biological weapons activities.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Confidential Information may be controlled by U.S. Export Regulations, and export, re-export or foreign disclosure (excluding to subsidiary employees) may require U.S. Government approval. The receiving party shall not use, export, transfer, make available or otherwise disclose any Confidential Information in violation of U.S. Export Regulations, including any use or development in nuclear, missile, chemical and/or biological weapons activities.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding subsidiary employees from export control restrictions violates U.S. Export Regulations by potentially allowing unauthorized access to controlled technology. This could lead to significant penalties and sanctions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR) control the export and re-export of sensitive technologies and information. By excluding subsidiary employees from export control restrictions, the changed text directly contradicts the EAR, which requires that access to controlled technologies be restricted based on nationality, location, and intended use, regardless of employment status. Violating the EAR can result in severe penalties, including fines, imprisonment, and denial of export privileges.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts U.S. Export Regulations by excluding subsidiary employees from export control restrictions, potentially allowing unauthorized access to controlled technology. The original text included subsidiary employees as part of export control restrictions while the changed text excluded them. The Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and scraped law snippet 2, which mentions the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), indicate that the change violates the EAR, which requires access to controlled technologies to be restricted based on nationality, location, and intended use, regardless of employment status. The scraped law snippet supports the reasoning that Subchapter C of Title 15, Commerce and Foreign Trade, Chapter VII covers Export Administration Regulations.
